tier2_short: The priest must complete the sacrifice by consuming both species.
tier2_explanation: The priest completes the Eucharistic sacrifice by consuming both species, acting as Christ the High Priest. Even when the faithful receive only the Host, the priest’s consumption fulfills ancient liturgical practice and the structure Christ established.
patristic_refs: Council of Trent citing earlier Fathers

title: Why are Eucharistic miracles necessary?
objection_text: Why are Eucharistic miracles necessary?
tier2_short: Eucharistic miracles strengthen faith in the Real Presence.
tier2_explanation: Eucharistic miracles aren’t required but occasionally given to strengthen faith. They highlight what is always true: Christ is present even when no visible sign appears. The Church carefully examines reported miracles to protect the integrity of belief.
patristic_refs: Augustine, Sermon 227

title: Why can the Mass be offered privately?
objection_text: Why can the Mass be offered privately?
tier2_short: The Mass benefits the whole Church even without a congregation.
tier2_explanation: The priest offers Christ’s sacrifice, which always has infinite value. A Mass without a congregation is still communal spiritually because it is offered for the whole Church. Early Christians recognized the priestly offering as ecclesial, not private.
patristic_refs: Cyprian, On the Lapsed

title: Is the Eucharist still valid if the priest is sinful?
objection_text: Is the Eucharist still valid if the priest is sinful?
tier2_short: Christ, not the priest’s holiness, ensures sacramental validity.
tier2_explanation: Validity depends on Christ’s power, not the priest’s personal holiness. This rejects Donatism, the ancient claim that sin invalidates sacraments. A sinful priest still consecrates validly, though he personally needs repentance.
patristic_refs: Augustine, Against the Donatists

title: Does receiving Communion in the hand disrespect the Eucharist?
objection_text: Does receiving Communion in the hand disrespect the Eucharist?
tier2_short: Both hand and tongue reception are reverent when done rightly.
tier2_explanation: The early Church often received in the hand with deep reverence. The Church allows both forms today, expecting devotion either way. Respect for the Eucharist is measured by faith and attitude, not by the method of reception.
patristic_refs: Cyril of Jerusalem, Catechesis 23

title: Justification by Faith Alone?
objection_text: We are justified by faith alone. Works play no role in salvation.
their_verses_text: Rom 3:28 – A man is justified by faith apart from works of the law. Rom 4:5 – Faith is credited as righteousness.
tier1_summary: Paul rejects works of the Mosaic law, not obedient faith.
tier1_verses_text: Jas 2:24 – A man is justified by works and not by faith alone. Gal 5:6 – Faith working through love.
tier2_short: Paul condemns works of the Mosaic law, not Christian obedience. Catholics affirm salvation by grace through faith, but a living faith must express itself in love. Scripture directly denies 'faith alone' and presents justification as ongoing and transformative.
tier2_explanation: Justification involves grace, faith, and cooperation in love.
tier3_discussion: If Scripture says 'not by faith alone,' why do Protestants affirm the opposite?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: 'He who created you without you will not justify you without you.'

title: Are Works 'Filthy Rags'?
objection_text: Human works are worthless; even our righteous acts are filthy rags.
their_verses_text: Isa 64:6 – All our righteous acts are like filthy rags.
tier1_summary: Isaiah describes unrepentant Israel, not grace-filled believers.
tier1_verses_text: Phil 2:12–13 – God works in us. Eph 2:10 – Created for good works.
tier2_short: Isaiah condemns hypocritical, unrepentant ritualism—not Spirit-empowered obedience. In Christ, works are God’s own work in us and therefore pleasing to Him. Scripture repeatedly affirms good works as part of God’s saving plan.
tier2_explanation: Grace makes works pleasing to God.
tier3_discussion: If works are worthless, why does Scripture say God prepared them for us to walk in?
patristic_refs: St. Irenaeus: Works are pleasing because done in faith.

title: Once Saved, Always Saved?
objection_text: A true believer cannot lose salvation.
their_verses_text: Jn 10:28 – No one snatches them from Christ. Rom 8:38–39 – Nothing separates us from Christ’s love.
tier1_summary: Security in Christ does not eliminate free will or the possibility of falling.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Cor 9:27 – Paul fears disqualification. Heb 6:4–6 – Believers can fall away.
tier2_short: Jesus’ promise protects against external forces, not freely chosen rejection. The NT repeatedly warns believers about falling away, showing salvation must be persevered in, not presumed.
tier2_explanation: Believers must persevere in grace.
tier3_discussion: If losing salvation is impossible, why warn about apostasy?
patristic_refs: Tertullian: We stand or fall by our own choice.

title: Does Baptism Save?
objection_text: Baptism is only a symbol; it does not save.
their_verses_text: 1 Cor 1:17 – Christ sent me to preach, not baptize.
tier1_summary: Scripture explicitly states that baptism saves.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Pet 3:21 – 'Baptism now saves you.' Jn 3:5 – Born of water and Spirit.
tier2_short: Paul contrasts baptizing personally with preaching—not baptism’s importance. The NT presents baptism as regenerative, united to the Spirit, and necessary for entrance into the Kingdom.
tier2_explanation: Baptism is a means of grace, not a symbol.
tier3_discussion: If baptism is symbolic, why does Scripture call it rebirth and salvation?
patristic_refs: St. Justin Martyr: Baptism is illumination.

title: Assurance of Salvation?
objection_text: We can know with certainty that we are saved.
their_verses_text: 1 Jn 5:13 – That you may know you have eternal life.
tier1_summary: Confidence is encouraged; absolute assurance is not biblical.
tier1_verses_text: Phil 2:12 – Work out salvation. 1 Cor 10:12 – Beware lest you fall.
tier2_short: Biblical 'knowing' means confidence in God’s promises, not infallible certainty of one’s future perseverance. Scripture repeatedly warns believers to remain vigilant.
tier2_explanation: Hope is sure; presumption is not.
tier3_discussion: If the apostles warn believers not to fall, how is absolute assurance possible?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Assurance is trust, not presumption.

title: Are Catholics Saved by Works?
objection_text: Catholics believe in works-based salvation, not grace.
their_verses_text: Eph 2:8–9 – Saved by grace through faith, not by works.
tier1_summary: Catholics affirm salvation by grace alone—works are grace-empowered cooperation.
tier1_verses_text: Phil 2:12–13 – God works in us. Jas 2:22 – Faith completed by works.
tier2_short: Catholic teaching denies salvation by human effort. Works are God’s action in us, expressing living faith. Grace initiates, empowers, and brings works to completion.
tier2_explanation: Grace makes our cooperation possible.
tier3_discussion: If works don’t matter, why does Scripture say they perfect faith?
patristic_refs: St. Clement: Justified by faith yet adorned by good works.

title: Can We Earn Salvation?
objection_text: Catholics teach salvation is earned by human effort.
their_verses_text: Titus 3:5 – Saved not by works of righteousness, but by His mercy.
tier1_summary: Salvation is entirely God’s gift—our cooperation is empowered by grace.
tier1_verses_text: Eph 2:8–10 – Saved by grace for good works. Phil 2:12–13 – God works in us to will and act.
tier2_short: Catholics do not earn salvation. Grace precedes, empowers, and completes every good action. Works are God’s work in us, not human achievement. Salvation is a gift that requires a free response but is never self-produced.
tier2_explanation: Grace initiates, accompanies, and completes our cooperation.
tier3_discussion: If works were purely human efforts, why does Scripture say God performs them in us?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: ‘When God rewards, He crowns His own gifts.’

title: Do Good Works Cancel Grace?
objection_text: If works are part of salvation, grace is no longer grace.
their_verses_text: Rom 11:6 – If by grace, not by works; otherwise grace is no longer grace.
tier1_summary: Paul contrasts grace with Mosaic works—not grace-filled obedience.
tier1_verses_text: Jas 2:22 – Faith completed by works. Gal 5:6 – Faith working through love.
tier2_short: Grace and works are not opposites. Grace produces works as its fruit. Paul rejects works of the Mosaic law, not Spirit-empowered Christian obedience. Scripture unites grace, faith, and works as part of salvation.
tier2_explanation: Grace transforms us into participants in God’s work.
tier3_discussion: If works contradict grace, why does grace produce works?
patristic_refs: St. Cyril: Grace makes possible what nature cannot.

title: Do Catholics Believe in Purgatory Because They Reject Christ’s Finished Work?
objection_text: Purgatory denies that Jesus fully paid for our sins.
their_verses_text: Jn 19:30 – ‘It is finished.’
tier1_summary: Christ’s work is complete—purgatory applies that grace, not adds to it.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Cor 3:13–15 – Saved, yet purified. Heb 12:14 – Need holiness to see God.
tier2_short: Purgatory applies Christ’s finished work by purifying the soul. No suffering adds to Calvary. Scripture shows a final purification for those saved but not yet perfected in holiness.
tier2_explanation: Purgatory completes sanctification through Christ’s grace alone.
tier3_discussion: If nothing unclean enters heaven, how are imperfect Christians perfected instantly?
patristic_refs: St. Gregory the Great describes purification after death.

title: Does God Predestine Some to Hell?
objection_text: Romans 9 teaches double predestination—God chooses who goes to heaven or hell.
their_verses_text: Rom 9:18 – God hardens whom He wills.
tier1_summary: Predestination affirms God’s initiative—not fatalism or coercion.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Tim 2:4 – God wills all to be saved. Ez 18:23 – God takes no pleasure in the death of the wicked.
tier2_short: Catholics affirm predestination, but not that God forces damnation. God foreknows free choices and offers sufficient grace to all. Scripture shows both divine sovereignty and real human freedom.
tier2_explanation: Predestination is God’s loving initiative, not fatalistic destiny.
tier3_discussion: If God forces damnation, why does Scripture say He desires all to be saved?
patristic_refs: St. John Chrysostom on free will and grace.

title: Is Repentance a Work?
objection_text: If repentance is required, salvation isn’t by faith alone.
their_verses_text: Acts 16:31 – Believe and be saved.
tier1_summary: Biblical faith includes repentance—a change of heart empowered by grace.
tier1_verses_text: Acts 2:38 – Repent and be baptized. Lk 13:3 – Unless you repent you will perish.
tier2_short: Repentance is not a human achievement but grace responding to grace. Scripture unites faith and repentance—never placing them in opposition. True faith includes turning from sin.
tier2_explanation: Repentance flows from grace as part of saving faith.
tier3_discussion: If repentance is unnecessary, why does Jesus command it repeatedly?
patristic_refs: St. Ambrose on repentance as healing grace.

title: Does God Justify the Ungodly Without Change?
objection_text: Justification is a legal declaration only; it does not transform the believer.
their_verses_text: Rom 4:5 – God justifies the ungodly.
tier1_summary: Justification is both a declaration and a transformation by grace.
tier1_verses_text: 2 Cor 5:17 – New creation in Christ. Titus 3:5–7 – Saved by regeneration and renewal.
tier2_short: Paul teaches that justification involves real inner renewal by the Holy Spirit, not only legal status. Catholics affirm God’s declaration and His transformation of the heart, united in the one act of justification.
tier2_explanation: God’s justification changes us into new creations.
tier3_discussion: If justification does not transform, why does Scripture describe it as rebirth and new creation?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Justification makes us righteous, not merely called righteous.

title: Is Justification a One-Time Event?
objection_text: Justification happens once; it is not a process.
their_verses_text: Rom 5:1 – Having been justified by faith…
tier1_summary: Scripture speaks of justification as past, present, and future.
tier1_verses_text: Phil 2:12 – Work out salvation. Rom 2:6–7 – God repays according to works.
tier2_short: Justification begins at conversion but continues through growth in righteousness. Scripture shows justification as ongoing and culminating in final judgment, not a single irreversible event.
tier2_explanation: Justification unfolds as we remain in grace.
tier3_discussion: If justification were purely one-time, why does Paul speak of attaining salvation?
patristic_refs: St. Cyril of Jerusalem describes justification as lifelong growth.

title: Does the Bible Teach Imputed Righteousness?
objection_text: Christ’s righteousness is imputed to believers; we remain unrighteous.
their_verses_text: 2 Cor 5:21 – We become the righteousness of God in Him.
tier1_summary: Scripture teaches imparted righteousness, not merely imputed righteousness.
tier1_verses_text: Rom 5:5 – Love poured into hearts. 1 Jn 3:7 – He who practices righteousness is righteous.
tier2_short: Imputed righteousness alone leaves believers unchanged. Scripture reveals that God pours His love into us, making us truly righteous by grace, not merely covering us with external righteousness.
tier2_explanation: Justification makes us inwardly righteous by grace.
tier3_discussion: If righteousness is only imputed, why does Scripture emphasize becoming righteous?
patristic_refs: St. Irenaeus: The Spirit makes us truly righteous.

title: Do Catholics Deny Christ as the Sole Mediator?
objection_text: Invoking saints or cooperating with grace denies Christ’s unique mediation.
their_verses_text: 1 Tim 2:5 – One mediator between God and man: Jesus Christ.
tier1_summary: Christ’s mediation enables all secondary participation, not excludes it.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Cor 3:9 – We are God’s fellow workers. Col 1:24 – Paul 'fills up what is lacking' in Christ’s sufferings.
tier2_short: Christ is the sole mediator by nature, but He shares His ministry with believers through grace. Intercessory prayer and cooperation do not replace Christ—they participate in His one mediation.
tier2_explanation: All mediation flows from Christ, not from human power.
tier3_discussion: If Christians cannot share in Christ’s work, why does Paul say we are co-workers?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Christ is the Mediator who includes us in His Body’s work.

title: Can Grace Be Resisted?
objection_text: God’s saving grace is irresistible to the elect.
their_verses_text: Jn 6:37 – All the Father gives me will come to me.
tier1_summary: Grace is powerful but not coercive; humans can resist God’s call.
tier1_verses_text: Acts 7:51 – 'You always resist the Holy Spirit.' Mt 23:37 – Jesus laments rejected grace.
tier2_short: Scripture shows God offering real grace even to those who refuse it. God desires all to be saved, yet respects human freedom. Irresistible grace contradicts the biblical portrayal of God’s universal invitation.
tier2_explanation: Grace invites, empowers, and respects freedom.
tier3_discussion: If grace is irresistible, why does Scripture show people resisting God?
patristic_refs: St. John Chrysostom emphasizes synergy of grace and free will.

title: Does God Choose Some People for Salvation Arbitrarily?
objection_text: Election is unconditional and unrelated to human response.
their_verses_text: Eph 1:4–5 – Chosen before the foundation of the world.
tier1_summary: God’s election is loving and relational, not mechanical or arbitrary.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Pet 1:2 – Elect according to foreknowledge. Rom 8:29 – Predestined for conformity to Christ.
tier2_short: Election reflects God’s loving plan and foreknowledge of human cooperation with grace. Scripture affirms both divine initiative and genuine human response—not mechanical selection of some and rejection of others.
tier2_explanation: Election is God’s loving call to relationship, not fatalistic selection.
tier3_discussion: If election is unconditional, why does Scripture tie it to foreknowledge and conformity?
patristic_refs: St. Justin Martyr affirms freedom and divine foreknowledge.

title: Does God Punish Us for Adam’s Sin?
objection_text: Original sin is unjust—why should we inherit guilt from Adam?
their_verses_text: Ez 18:20 – The soul who sins shall die; a child shall not bear the guilt of a parent.
tier1_summary: We inherit wounded nature, not personal guilt; Christ heals what Adam wounded.
tier1_verses_text: Rom 5:12–19 – Through one man, many were made sinners. 1 Cor 15:22 – In Adam all die; in Christ all shall be made alive.
tier2_short: Original sin is not personal guilt but a wounded nature lacking original holiness. We inherit consequences, not blame. Christ restores what Adam lost. Salvation heals and elevates us by grace.
tier2_explanation: Original sin explains our wounded nature and need for Christ.
tier3_discussion: If Adam’s fall does not affect us, why does Scripture contrast Adam and Christ?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Adam’s fall affects all, but guilt is personal.

title: Are Sacraments Additions to the Gospel?
objection_text: Salvation is by faith alone—sacraments add human works to the gospel.
their_verses_text: Gal 1:8 – Warning against another gospel.
tier1_summary: Sacraments are Christ’s actions, not human inventions.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 20:22–23 – Authority to forgive sins. Mt 28:19 – Command to baptize.
tier2_short: Sacraments don't compete with grace—they deliver it. Christ instituted baptism, Eucharist, confession, and more. These are His works through His Church, not human additions.
tier2_explanation: Sacraments communicate grace won by Christ.
tier3_discussion: If sacraments distort the gospel, why did Christ institute them?
patristic_refs: St. Ignatius speaks of the Eucharist as the medicine of immortality.

title: Do Catholics Believe Suffering Earns Salvation?
objection_text: Catholics teach that suffering contributes to salvation, replacing Christ’s work.
their_verses_text: Heb 10:14 – By one sacrifice He has perfected forever those being sanctified.
tier1_summary: Christ’s sacrifice is sufficient; our suffering participates in His, not adds to it.
tier1_verses_text: Col 1:24 – Share in Christ’s sufferings. Rom 8:17 – Joint heirs if we suffer with Him.
tier2_short: Christ’s sacrifice is once for all. Our suffering unites us to His and applies His grace to our lives. Participation does not replace the Cross; it flows from it and conforms us to Christ.
tier2_explanation: Christian suffering applies Christ’s grace, not adds to His sacrifice.
tier3_discussion: If suffering is meaningless, why does Scripture link it to glorification?
patristic_refs: St. Ignatius: ‘Let me be ground by the teeth of beasts to become pure bread.’

title: Is Salvation Instant or Lifelong?
objection_text: Christ completed salvation on the Cross—we contribute nothing further.
their_verses_text: Jn 19:30 – ‘It is finished.’
tier1_summary: Christ finished redemption; salvation applies that redemption over time.
tier1_verses_text: Phil 1:6 – God completes the work in us. Heb 10:14 – Being sanctified.
tier2_short: Christ finished the work of redemption, but salvation includes justification, sanctification, and final glorification. Scripture speaks of being saved, having been saved, and will be saved.
tier2_explanation: Salvation is applied through time, though won once for all.
tier3_discussion: If salvation is instant, why does Scripture describe our being sanctified?
patristic_refs: St. Gregory Nazianzen: Salvation is a process of becoming like God.

title: Does God Love Only the Elect?
objection_text: Scripture says God loves the elect in a special saving way, not all people.
their_verses_text: Jn 10:26 – You do not believe because you are not my sheep.
tier1_summary: God’s saving will is universal; grace does not exclude anyone.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Tim 2:4 – God wills all to be saved. 1 Jn 2:2 – Christ died for the whole world.
tier2_short: God’s love is universal. Election is relational, not selective favoritism. Christ died for all, though not all accept His grace. Scripture rejects the idea that God withholds saving love from most people.
tier2_explanation: God loves all and offers grace universally.
tier3_discussion: If God loves only the elect, why does Scripture say Christ died for the world?
patristic_refs: St. John Chrysostom teaches God draws all through grace.

title: Why Confess Sins to a Priest?
objection_text: We confess directly to God—confessing to a priest is unnecessary for salvation.
their_verses_text: 1 Jn 1:9 – If we confess our sins, He forgives us.
tier1_summary: Christ gave priests authority to forgive sins sacramentally.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 20:21–23 – Sins forgiven or retained. Jas 5:16 – Confess sins to one another.
tier2_short: Confession to God is essential, but Christ instituted priestly absolution as a channel of grace. Priests act in Christ’s name, applying His forgiveness sacramentally for healing and reconciliation.
tier2_explanation: Confession applies Christ’s forgiveness through His ministers.
tier3_discussion: If confession to a priest is unnecessary, why did Christ give authority to forgive sins?
patristic_refs: St. Cyprian and early Fathers affirm priestly absolution.

title: Are We Saved by Calling on Jesus’ Name Alone?
objection_text: Romans 10:13 says calling on Jesus’ name is enough for salvation.
their_verses_text: Rom 10:13 – Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.
tier1_summary: Calling on Christ expresses faith—but Scripture shows salvation involves repentance, baptism, and obedience.
tier1_verses_text: Acts 2:21,38 – Call on the Lord and be baptized. Mt 7:21 – Not everyone who says ‘Lord’ enters the Kingdom.
tier2_short: Calling on Jesus is the beginning of salvation, not its completion. Scripture unites calling, repenting, believing, and being baptized. Jesus warns that verbal confession alone does not save without obedience.
tier2_explanation: Calling on Christ initiates salvation but must be lived out.
tier3_discussion: If calling alone saves, why does Jesus say obedience is required?
patristic_refs: St. Justin Martyr: Faith and baptism together initiate salvation.

title: Is Baptism Necessary if the Thief on the Cross Was Saved Without It?
objection_text: The thief on the cross proves baptism is unnecessary for salvation.
their_verses_text: Lk 23:42–43 – Jesus promises paradise to the repentant thief.
tier1_summary: God is not bound by His sacraments—we are.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 3:5 – Born of water and Spirit. Acts 2:38 – Repent and be baptized.
tier2_short: The thief received an extraordinary grace before the New Covenant sacraments were instituted. Baptism is normally necessary because Christ commands it, but God can operate outside His sacraments in exceptional cases.
tier2_explanation: The thief is an exception, not the norm Christ established.
tier3_discussion: If baptism is unnecessary, why does Christ command it for all disciples?
patristic_refs: St. Ambrose: The thief was saved by desire for baptism.

title: Does God Reject People Who Haven’t Heard the Gospel?
objection_text: Only explicit faith in Jesus saves—unreached people are damned.
their_verses_text: Jn 14:6 – No one comes to the Father except through me.
tier1_summary: All are saved through Christ, but not all must know Him explicitly.
tier1_verses_text: Rom 2:14–16 – Law written on the heart. 1 Tim 2:4 – God wills all to be saved.
tier2_short: Christ alone saves, yet Scripture teaches God judges based on the light a person receives. Those ignorant of Christ through no fault of their own may respond to grace implicitly.
tier2_explanation: Christ saves all, but knowledge of Him may be implicit.
tier3_discussion: If God desires all to be saved, how can He condemn those who never heard Him?
patristic_refs: St. Justin Martyr: Seeds of the Word are in all peoples.

title: Does Moral Law Save or Condemn?
objection_text: The law condemns—so why obey commandments if we are under grace?
their_verses_text: Rom 6:14 – You are not under law but under grace.
tier1_summary: Grace empowers us to fulfill God’s moral law, not ignore it.
tier1_verses_text: Rom 3:31 – We uphold the law. Jn 14:15 – If you love me, keep my commandments.
tier2_short: Grace liberates from the Mosaic law’s condemnatory power but empowers obedience to Christ’s commandments. Salvation is not lawlessness—grace transforms us so we can live the moral law.
tier2_explanation: Grace fulfills the law by transforming our hearts.
tier3_discussion: If grace removes obligation, why does Jesus insist on commandments?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Grace enables what the law commands.

title: Are Good Works Just Evidence, Not a Condition of Salvation?
objection_text: Works prove faith but play no role in salvation itself.
their_verses_text: Mt 7:17–20 – Good trees bear good fruit.
tier1_summary: Works are necessary for salvation because they perfect faith.
tier1_verses_text: Jas 2:24 – Not by faith alone. Mt 25 – Judgment based on works of mercy.
tier2_short: Works are more than evidence—they complete and make faith alive. Scripture explicitly ties final judgment to deeds and denies salvation by faith alone. Works are grace-enabled cooperation with Christ.
tier2_explanation: Works are grace-filled acts that complete faith.
tier3_discussion: If works merely show salvation, why does Jesus judge based on them?
patristic_refs: St. Polycarp: We are saved if we walk worthy of His commandments.

title: Does Fear of the Lord Contradict Salvation by Grace?
objection_text: Fear has no place in salvation—love removes fear.
their_verses_text: 1 Jn 4:18 – Perfect love casts out fear.
tier1_summary: Scripture distinguishes servile fear from filial fear—love deepens the right kind of fear.
tier1_verses_text: Phil 2:12 – Work out salvation with fear. Prov 1:7 – Fear of the Lord is beginning of wisdom.
tier2_short: Perfect love removes fear of punishment, but not reverent awe. Scripture commands holy fear even for believers. Filial fear strengthens love, guards against sin, and deepens trust.
tier2_explanation: Filial fear accompanies love and protects the soul.
tier3_discussion: If all fear is gone, why does the New Testament command holy fear?
patristic_refs: St. Basil: Fear begins the journey; love perfects it.

title: Does God Forgive Without Repentance?
objection_text: Since salvation is a free gift, repentance is unnecessary.
their_verses_text: Eph 2:8–9 – Saved by grace through faith, not by works.
tier1_summary: Grace saves, but Christ requires repentance as part of faith.
tier1_verses_text: Lk 13:3 – Unless you repent you will perish. Acts 2:38 – Repent and be baptized.
tier2_short: Grace initiates salvation, but Jesus explicitly commands repentance. Biblical faith includes turning away from sin. Repentance is not a work but a grace-enabled response that aligns the heart with God.
tier2_explanation: Repentance is a grace-enabled turning toward God.
tier3_discussion: If repentance is unnecessary, why does Jesus make it a condition for salvation?
patristic_refs: St. Ambrose: Repentance restores the heart to grace.

title: Are All Sins Equal Before God?
objection_text: All sin is the same—no distinction exists between mortal and venial sin.
their_verses_text: Jas 2:10 – Whoever keeps the whole law yet fails in one point is guilty of all.
tier1_summary: Scripture distinguishes serious sins that lead to death from lesser sins.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Jn 5:16–17 – Sins leading to death vs. not. Lk 12:47–48 – Different degrees of guilt.
tier2_short: James teaches that any sin ruptures our relationship with God, but Scripture clearly distinguishes grave sins from lesser ones. Catholic teaching reflects this biblical distinction.
tier2_explanation: Serious sins cause spiritual death; lesser sins wound but do not sever.
tier3_discussion: If all sins are equal, why does Scripture name ‘sins unto death’?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Some sins kill the soul; others do not.

title: Does Predestination Override Free Will?
objection_text: If God predestines people, free will is irrelevant.
their_verses_text: Jn 6:44 – No one comes unless the Father draws him.
tier1_summary: God draws all, yet the human response remains free.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Tim 2:4 – God wills all to be saved. Mt 23:37 – Jesus laments resisted grace.
tier2_short: God initiates salvation by grace, but humans must freely cooperate. Scripture affirms both divine initiative and human responsibility, rejecting fatalistic interpretations of predestination.
tier2_explanation: Predestination includes free cooperation with grace.
tier3_discussion: If free will is irrelevant, why does Jesus lament people refusing Him?
patristic_refs: St. John Chrysostom on synergy between grace and freedom.

title: Is Faith Alone Enough Without Charity?
objection_text: Faith alone saves; works of charity are optional.
their_verses_text: Jn 3:16 – Whoever believes has eternal life.
tier1_summary: Saving faith expresses itself through charity and obedience.
tier1_verses_text: Gal 5:6 – Faith working through love. Jas 2:17 – Faith without works is dead.
tier2_short: Biblical faith is living, obedient, and expressed through love. Scripture rejects a faith that lacks charity. Salvation requires grace-enabled faith expressed in love.
tier2_explanation: Charity is the form and perfection of saving faith.
tier3_discussion: If faith alone saves, why does Scripture deny salvation to loveless faith?
patristic_refs: St. Gregory the Great: Faith without love is empty.

title: Does God Refuse Forgiveness After Repeated Sin?
objection_text: After repeated sin, God eventually refuses forgiveness.
their_verses_text: Heb 10:26 – No sacrifice remains for willful sinning.
tier1_summary: God always forgives the repentant; this verse warns against hardened rejection.
tier1_verses_text: Lk 15:7 – Joy over one repentant sinner. 1 Jn 1:9 – God forgives and cleanses.
tier2_short: Hebrews warns against persistent, unrepentant rejection—not weakness or struggle. God never refuses genuine repentance. Mercy remains open throughout life for those who turn back to Him.
tier2_explanation: God forgives without limit when repentance is sincere.
tier3_discussion: If God refuses repentance, why does Christ command forgiveness ‘seventy times seven’?
patristic_refs: St. John Chrysostom: The door of repentance is always open.

title: Are Sacraments Invalid Because Humans Are Sinful?
objection_text: A sinful priest cannot validly administer sacraments.
their_verses_text: Mt 7:18–20 – A bad tree cannot bear good fruit.
tier1_summary: Christ works through the sacraments regardless of the minister’s holiness.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 20:21–23 – Sins forgiven or retained. 2 Cor 4:7 – Treasure in earthen vessels.
tier2_short: Sacraments draw their power from Christ, not the priest. The holiness of the minister affects his soul, not the sacrament’s validity. This reflects the ancient doctrine *ex opere operato.*
tier2_explanation: Christ—not the priest—confers sacramental grace.
tier3_discussion: If sacraments depend on human holiness, how can any be certain of grace?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine refuted the Donatists on this point decisively.

title: Does God Predestine People to Heaven Without Their Consent?
objection_text: If salvation is God’s choice, human consent is irrelevant.
their_verses_text: Rom 9:16 – It depends not on human will or exertion, but on God.
tier1_summary: God’s initiative begins salvation, but He never overrides human freedom.
tier1_verses_text: Dt 30:19 – Choose life. Rev 3:20 – Christ knocks; we must open.
tier2_short: Predestination affirms God’s initiative, not coercion. Scripture shows salvation requires a free response to grace. God desires cooperation, not passive recipients.
tier2_explanation: Predestination invites, but never forces, our consent.
tier3_discussion: If our choice doesn’t matter, why does Scripture command us to choose?
patristic_refs: St. John Damascene: God foreknows but does not predetermine our choices.

title: Does Salvation Exclude the Possibility of Falling Away?
objection_text: True believers cannot fall away from salvation.
their_verses_text: 1 Jn 2:19 – Those who left were never truly of us.
tier1_summary: Scripture warns believers repeatedly about falling away.
tier1_verses_text: Heb 10:26–29 – Willful sin after knowing truth. 1 Cor 9:27 – Paul fears disqualification.
tier2_short: Apostasy is possible even for genuine believers, as Scripture warns. God is faithful, but humans can reject grace freely at any time.
tier2_explanation: Believers must persevere; salvation can be forfeited.
tier3_discussion: If falling away is impossible, why does Scripture warn against it so often?
patristic_refs: St. Clement warns believers to remain steadfast.

title: Does Confession to God Alone Replace Sacramental Confession?
objection_text: We confess to God privately; a priest is unnecessary.
their_verses_text: Ps 51:4 – Against you only have I sinned.
tier1_summary: Scripture shows God forgives through ordained ministers.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 20:23 – Authority to forgive or retain sins. Jas 5:16 – Confess sins to one another.
tier2_short: Private confession to God is essential, but Christ instituted sacramental confession to bring His forgiveness through the Church. Priests act in His name to reconcile sinners.
tier2_explanation: Sacramental confession applies Christ’s forgiveness visibly.
tier3_discussion: If priestly forgiveness is unnecessary, why did Christ authorize it explicitly?
patristic_refs: St. Cyprian: Forgiveness is given through the bishops and priests.

title: Does Christ’s Atonement Remove All Need for Penance?
objection_text: Jesus paid it all—there is no need for penance or spiritual discipline.
their_verses_text: Jn 19:30 – ‘It is finished.’
tier1_summary: Christ’s atonement is complete, but discipleship requires ongoing conversion.
tier1_verses_text: Lk 9:23 – Take up your cross daily. 2 Cor 7:1 – Perfect holiness in fear of God.
tier2_short: Penance does not add to Christ’s sacrifice; it applies the grace of conversion to heal the effects of sin. Discipline transforms the heart and conforms believers to Christ.
tier2_explanation: Penance flows from grace and deepens conversion.
tier3_discussion: If no discipline is needed, why does Jesus command daily self-denial?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Penance heals the wounds sin leaves behind.

title: Are Good Works Opposed to Grace?
objection_text: Grace and works are mutually exclusive; works destroy grace.
their_verses_text: Rom 11:6 – If by grace, it cannot be by works.
tier1_summary: Paul rejects works of the Mosaic law, not grace-filled obedience.
tier1_verses_text: Eph 2:10 – Created for good works. Jas 2:22 – Faith completed by works.
tier2_short: Grace empowers good works; works do not compete with grace but express it. Scripture never opposes grace and obedience—they are inseparable aspects of salvation.
tier2_explanation: Grace produces good works as its natural fruit.
tier3_discussion: If works contradict grace, why does Scripture say grace creates good works?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: God crowns His own gifts in us.

title: Do Catholics Believe They Can Lose Salvation Through Mortal Sin?
objection_text: If salvation is by grace, mortal sin cannot cause someone to lose it.
their_verses_text: Jn 10:28 – Nothing can snatch them out of my hand.
tier1_summary: Nothing external can separate us from Christ—but we can freely reject Him.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Jn 5:16–17 – Sin leading to death. Heb 6:4–6 – Falling away after enlightenment.
tier2_short: God preserves us, but salvation remains relational; serious sin freely chosen can sever that relationship. Scripture distinguishes mortal sin that kills the soul from lesser sins.
tier2_explanation: Mortal sin rejects grace and ruptures communion with Christ.
tier3_discussion: If mortal sin can’t sever salvation, why does Scripture speak of sins ‘unto death’?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Grave sin destroys charity in the soul.

title: Does Salvation Happen Without the Church?
objection_text: The Church is irrelevant—salvation is strictly individual.
their_verses_text: Mt 18:20 – Where two or three gather, God is present.
tier1_summary: Scripture shows Christ saves us *as members of His Body*, the Church.
tier1_verses_text: Eph 1:22–23 – The Church is Christ’s Body. Acts 2:47 – The Lord added to the Church those being saved.
tier2_short: Salvation is personal, not private. Christ established a visible Church as the means through which salvation is proclaimed, celebrated, and lived. Scripture teaches we are saved *into* Christ’s Body, not apart from it.
tier2_explanation: Salvation incorporates us into the Church Christ founded.
tier3_discussion: If the Church is irrelevant, why does Scripture say salvation adds us to it?
patristic_refs: St. Cyprian: ‘You cannot have God as Father without the Church as Mother.’

title: Can Someone Be Saved Without Water Baptism?
objection_text: Physical baptism is unnecessary—only spiritual baptism matters.
their_verses_text: Jn 4:24 – Worship in spirit and truth.
tier1_summary: Jesus teaches new birth requires water *and* Spirit—not Spirit alone.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 3:5 – Born of water and Spirit. Acts 8 – Eunuch asks for water baptism.
tier2_short: The New Testament consistently unites water and Spirit in Christian rebirth. Desire for baptism may suffice in extraordinary circumstances, but Christ established sacramental baptism as the normative means of salvation.
tier2_explanation: Baptism of desire exists, but water baptism is the norm.
tier3_discussion: If water baptism is optional, why was every convert in Acts baptized?
patristic_refs: St. Justin Martyr: Baptism is illumination and regeneration.

title: Can a Christian Live in Serious Sin and Still Be Saved?
objection_text: Once saved, behavior doesn’t affect salvation.
their_verses_text: Jn 5:24 – Passed from death to life.
tier1_summary: Scripture warns that persistent grave sin excludes people from the Kingdom.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Cor 6 – Sinners will not inherit the Kingdom. Gal 5 – Works of the flesh exclude from the Kingdom.
tier2_short: Grace transforms, not excuses, sin. Scripture clearly teaches that choosing serious sin breaks communion with God. Salvation is relational; it demands ongoing fidelity to Christ.
tier2_explanation: Grave sin freely chosen can forfeit salvation.
tier3_discussion: If sin can’t affect salvation, why does Scripture list sins that exclude?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Mortal sin destroys charity in the soul.

title: Are Good Intentions Enough for Salvation?
objection_text: As long as you mean well, God accepts you.
their_verses_text: 1 Sam 16:7 – Man looks at appearance; God looks at the heart.
tier1_summary: Good intentions are insufficient—Jesus commands obedience to His teachings.
tier1_verses_text: Mt 7:21 – Not everyone who says ‘Lord’ enters. Jn 14:15 – If you love me, keep my commandments.
tier2_short: God sees the heart, but true love expresses itself in obedience. Salvation requires grace-enabled faith, not mere sincerity. Jesus warns that good intentions alone do not save.
tier2_explanation: Sincerity matters, but obedience and grace are essential.
tier3_discussion: If good intentions save, why does Jesus deny salvation to the sincerely disobedient?
patristic_refs: St. Gregory: Love proves itself through action.

title: Does God Condemn People for Ignorance?
objection_text: Those who don’t know Christ are automatically condemned.
their_verses_text: Jn 3:18 – Whoever does not believe is condemned already.
tier1_summary: Scripture distinguishes voluntary unbelief from invincible ignorance.
tier1_verses_text: Lk 12:47–48 – Degrees of responsibility. Rom 2 – Law on the heart.
tier2_short: Christ alone saves, but God judges people based on the truth they actually knew. Those ignorant of Christ through no fault of their own may respond to grace implicitly. Condemnation follows willful rejection, not unavoidable ignorance.
tier2_explanation: God condemns rejection of grace—not ignorance of facts.
tier3_discussion: If ignorance condemns, why does Scripture teach differing degrees of responsibility?
patristic_refs: St. Justin: Seeds of the Word are in every heart.

title: Does God’s Mercy Mean Everyone Will Be Saved?
objection_text: A loving God would never allow anyone to go to hell.
their_verses_text: 1 Tim 2:4 – God desires all to be saved.
tier1_summary: God desires universal salvation, but He honors human freedom.
tier1_verses_text: Mt 25:46 – Eternal punishment. 2 Pt 3:9 – God patient, not wishing any to perish.
tier2_short: Hell exists because God respects human freedom. Salvation is offered to all, but not all accept it. Scripture affirms both God’s universal salvific will and the real possibility of eternal rejection.
tier2_explanation: God offers salvation universally but never forces acceptance.
tier3_discussion: If everyone is saved automatically, why did Jesus warn so often about hell?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: God created us without us, but will not save us without us.

title: Is Membership in a Denomination Irrelevant for Salvation?
objection_text: All that matters is believing in Jesus; church affiliation is irrelevant.
their_verses_text: Rom 10:9 – If you confess Jesus and believe, you will be saved.
tier1_summary: Belief unites us to Christ’s Body, the Church—never to isolation.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Cor 12:13 – Baptized into one Body. Eph 4 – One Body, one faith, one baptism.
tier2_short: Faith unites believers into Christ’s Body, not separate groups. Scripture shows salvation brings believers into visible communion, not independent Christianity.
tier2_explanation: Salvation incorporates believers into Christ’s one visible Body.
tier3_discussion: If affiliation is irrelevant, why does Scripture insist on one Body and one faith?
patristic_refs: St. Ireneaus emphasizes unity in the visible Church.

title: Does God Overlook Sin Because He Is Loving?
objection_text: A loving God wouldn’t hold people accountable for sins.
their_verses_text: 1 Jn 4:8 – God is love.
tier1_summary: God’s love includes justice—He heals sin; He does not excuse it.
tier1_verses_text: Heb 12:6 – The Lord disciplines whom He loves. Rom 2:6 – God repays according to works.
tier2_short: Love does not eliminate moral accountability. God forgives sin, but He also calls for repentance and transformation. Divine love is healing, not permissive.
tier2_explanation: Love heals and transforms; it does not dismiss sin.
tier3_discussion: If love excuses sin, why does Scripture pair God’s love with discipline?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: God loves us too much to leave us in our sins.

title: Is Fear of Judgment Unnecessary for Believers?
objection_text: Believers should never fear God’s judgment.
their_verses_text: Rom 8:1 – No condemnation for those in Christ.
tier1_summary: Confidence in Christ coexists with reverence and accountability.
tier1_verses_text: 2 Cor 5:10 – All must appear before judgment. Phil 2:12 – Work out salvation with fear.
tier2_short: Believers have confidence in Christ’s mercy, yet Scripture teaches ongoing accountability. Holy fear protects against complacency and deepens humility.
tier2_explanation: Confidence and holy fear work together for salvation.
tier3_discussion: If believers face no judgment, why does Scripture say all must stand before Christ?
patristic_refs: St. Basil: Holy fear guards the soul.

title: Does Grace Mean Effort Is Unimportant?
objection_text: Effort in the Christian life doesn’t matter—grace does everything.
their_verses_text: Eph 2:8–9 – Saved by grace, not works.
tier1_summary: Grace empowers effort—it does not eliminate it.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Cor 15:10 – Grace and hard work together. 2 Pt 1 – Add virtue, knowledge, self-control.
tier2_short: Grace produces transformation, not passivity. Scripture teaches believers must cooperate in growth through grace. Christian effort is participation in God’s work.
tier2_explanation: Grace makes effort fruitful and meaningful.
tier3_discussion: If effort is irrelevant, why does Scripture command believers to grow in virtue?
patristic_refs: St. Gregory: Grace perfects effort, not replaces it.

title: Is Salvation Purely Spiritual, Not Bodily?
objection_text: God saves souls, not bodies—physical works and sacraments don’t matter.
their_verses_text: Jn 6:63 – The Spirit gives life; the flesh is of no avail.
tier1_summary: God created and redeems both body and soul; salvation is incarnational.
tier1_verses_text: Rom 8:11 – God gives life to mortal bodies. 1 Cor 6 – Your body is a temple.
tier2_short: Christian salvation is bodily and spiritual. The Incarnation reveals that God works through physical realities. Sacraments fit this biblical pattern of God using matter to convey grace.
tier2_explanation: Salvation redeems both body and soul.
tier3_discussion: If salvation is purely spiritual, why does Scripture promise bodily resurrection?
patristic_refs: St. Irenaeus defends bodily resurrection against Gnostics.

title: Is Emotional Experience Proof of Salvation?
objection_text: Feeling saved is evidence of salvation.
their_verses_text: Rom 8:16 – The Spirit testifies that we are God’s children.
tier1_summary: The Spirit’s witness is deeper than emotion; salvation is evidenced by life in Christ.
tier1_verses_text: Mt 7:20 – Know them by their fruits. Gal 5 – Fruit of the Spirit.
tier2_short: Emotions fluctuate and cannot guarantee salvation. Scripture identifies the true signs of salvation as ongoing conversion, obedience, and the fruits of the Spirit—not subjective feelings.
tier2_explanation: Salvation is shown through transformed life, not emotion.
tier3_discussion: If feelings prove salvation, why does Scripture insist on fruits and obedience?
patristic_refs: St. Anthony the Great: Do not trust feelings—look for virtue.

title: Does God Punish People Eternally for Temporal Sins?
objection_text: Eternal punishment is unjust for sins committed in time.
their_verses_text: Rom 6:23 – The wages of sin is death.
tier1_summary: Scripture teaches that mortal sin ruptures communion with God, who is eternal.
tier1_verses_text: Mt 25:46 – Eternal punishment or life. Heb 9:27 – Judgment follows death.
tier2_short: Sin against an infinite God has eternal consequences because it rejects eternal life. Hell is not a sentence imposed arbitrarily but the final ratification of a person’s freely chosen separation from God.
tier2_explanation: Eternal loss reflects a freely chosen eternal rejection of God.
tier3_discussion: If eternal punishment is unjust, why does Jesus explicitly teach it?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Hell is the consequence of choosing separation from God.

title: Is Salvation Lost Every Time We Sin?
objection_text: If Catholics believe in mortal sin, then every sin threatens salvation.
their_verses_text: Rom 3:23 – All have sinned.
tier1_summary: Scripture distinguishes sins that kill the soul from lesser sins.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Jn 5:16–17 – Mortal vs. venial sin. Lk 12 – Different degrees of guilt.
tier2_short: Catholic teaching reflects Scripture: only grave sin freely chosen destroys charity in the soul. Venial sins wound but do not sever relationship with God.
tier2_explanation: Not all sins are mortal; only grave sin destroys charity.
tier3_discussion: If all sins end salvation, why does Scripture distinguish sins unto death?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Mortal sin kills charity; lesser sins do not.

title: Is Final Judgment Based Only on Faith?
objection_text: Believers are judged only on whether they accepted Jesus.
their_verses_text: Jn 3:18 – Whoever believes is not condemned.
tier1_summary: Scripture teaches judgment includes deeds, not faith alone.
tier1_verses_text: Mt 25 – Judgment on works of mercy. Rom 2 – God repays according to deeds.
tier2_short: Final judgment reflects both faith and the works that flow from it. Scripture consistently links salvation to perseverance, charity, and obedience—not verbal profession alone.
tier2_explanation: Final judgment evaluates faith expressed in works.
tier3_discussion: If only belief matters, why does Jesus judge based on charity?
patristic_refs: St. Justin: We are judged by faith and works together.

title: Do Good Works Steal Glory From God?
objection_text: If works matter, humans get credit instead of God.
their_verses_text: Isa 42:8 – My glory I give to no other.
tier1_summary: All good works are God’s gifts operating within us.
tier1_verses_text: Phil 2 – God works in us. Eph 2 – Created for good works.
tier2_short: Good works do not compete with God’s glory; they manifest it. When we cooperate with grace, God is glorified in our transformation and obedience.
tier2_explanation: Works reveal God’s glory by displaying His grace in us.
tier3_discussion: If works steal glory, why does Jesus command them?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: God crowns His own gifts in us.

title: Does Human Weakness Prove Salvation Cannot Be Lost?
objection_text: Because believers struggle with sin, they must remain saved regardless.
their_verses_text: Rom 7:19 – I do not do the good I want.
tier1_summary: Struggle is normal—deliberate grave sin still destroys charity.
tier1_verses_text: Gal 5 – Works of flesh exclude. Eph 4:30 – Do not grieve the Spirit.
tier2_short: Ongoing struggle does not mean sin is harmless. Scripture warns that freely chosen grave sin separates us from God, even while weakness and temptation remain part of Christian life.
tier2_explanation: Weakness is not damnation; grave sin is.
tier3_discussion: If weakness guarantees salvation, why does Paul warn of sins that exclude?
patristic_refs: St. John Chrysostom: Distinguish weakness from willful rebellion.

title: Is Baptism Valid Without Understanding?
objection_text: A person must understand and consciously accept baptism for it to save.
their_verses_text: Mk 16:16 – Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved.
tier1_summary: Grace precedes understanding—God works sacramentally even in infants.
tier1_verses_text: Acts 2 – Promise for you and your children. Col 2 – Baptism replaces circumcision.
tier2_short: Understanding is valuable but not required for the sacrament’s validity. Baptism, like circumcision, incorporates the person into God’s covenant family regardless of cognitive maturity.
tier2_explanation: God acts in baptism independent of intellectual capacity.
tier3_discussion: If maturity is required, why did God command circumcision of infants?
patristic_refs: St. Polycarp: Infants included in God’s covenant people.

title: Does the Thief on the Cross Disprove Sacraments?
objection_text: The thief was saved without baptism, confession, or works—so sacraments are unnecessary.
their_verses_text: Lk 23:43 – “Today you will be with me in Paradise.”
tier1_summary: God is not bound by the sacraments; we are.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 3:5 – Born of water and Spirit. Acts 10 – No one can withhold baptism.
tier2_short: The thief represents extraordinary grace, not the norm. Christ instituted sacraments as ordinary means of salvation, though He can save apart from them when circumstances make them impossible.
tier2_explanation: Exceptions prove God’s freedom, not the abolition of sacraments.
tier3_discussion: If sacraments are unnecessary, why did Jesus command baptism?
patristic_refs: St. Ambrose: Desire for baptism can suffice in extremis.

title: Is Repentance Merely Feeling Sorry?
objection_text: Repentance is just an emotional moment—change isn’t required.
their_verses_text: Ps 51:17 – A broken spirit God will not despise.
tier1_summary: Repentance includes conversion, not just emotion.
tier1_verses_text: Acts 26 – Perform deeds worthy of repentance. Mt 3 – Bear fruit that shows repentance.
tier2_short: Scripture connects repentance with concrete change. Genuine repentance includes sorrow, confession, and turning away from sin by grace—not merely feeling bad.
tier2_explanation: Repentance is transformation, not emotion alone.
tier3_discussion: If repentance needs no change, why does Scripture demand fruit?
patristic_refs: St. John Chrysostom: Repentance is a change of life.

title: Is Emotional Assurance the Same as Saving Faith?
objection_text: Feeling secure means you truly believe.
their_verses_text: Rom 8:16 – The Spirit testifies that we are God’s children.
tier1_summary: Saving faith expresses itself through obedience and perseverance.
tier1_verses_text: Jas 2 – Not by faith alone. Heb 10 – Need endurance to receive the promise.
tier2_short: Assurance is a gift, but emotions fluctuate. Scripture links authentic faith to perseverance and obedience—not to subjective feelings.
tier2_explanation: True faith endures; emotions come and go.
tier3_discussion: If emotions prove salvation, why do even saints experience darkness?
patristic_refs: St. Teresa of Ávila: Faith persists even without consolations.

title: Does Predestination Make Prayer Useless?
objection_text: If God predestines salvation, prayer changes nothing.
their_verses_text: Eph 1:11 – God works all things according to His will.
tier1_summary: God wills not only the ends but the means—including prayer.
tier1_verses_text: Jas 5 – Prayer is powerful. 1 Tim 2 – Pray for all; God desires all saved.
tier2_short: Predestination includes our cooperation. Prayer unites us to God’s will and participates in His saving plan. Scripture shows prayer truly affects outcomes within God’s providence.
tier2_explanation: Prayer is part of God’s saving plan, not opposed to it.
tier3_discussion: If prayer is useless, why does Scripture command it constantly?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: God grants some things only through prayer.

title: Does Infant Salvation Require Explicit Belief?
objection_text: Infants cannot believe, so they cannot be saved.
their_verses_text: Mk 16:16 – Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved.
tier1_summary: God saves by grace, not intellectual capacity.
tier1_verses_text: Lk 18 – Let the children come. Acts 2 – Promise for you and your children.
tier2_short: Jesus welcomes infants into the kingdom. Salvation depends on God’s grace and covenant, not cognitive ability. Baptism initiates infants into Christ’s saving work.
tier2_explanation: Grace, not intellect, is required for salvation.
tier3_discussion: If infants cannot receive grace, why does Jesus bless them?
patristic_refs: St. Irenaeus notes infants are regenerated in Christ.

title: Does God’s Foreknowledge Force Our Choices?
objection_text: If God knows the future, we do not act freely.
their_verses_text: Ps 139:4 – God knows every word before it is spoken.
tier1_summary: Foreknowledge does not equal predetermination.
tier1_verses_text: Dt 30 – Choose life. Sir 15 – God set before man life and death.
tier2_short: Knowing an action is not the same as causing it. Scripture affirms both divine omniscience and real human freedom. God foreknows free choices without forcing them.
tier2_explanation: God knows our choices but does not cause them.
tier3_discussion: If foreknowledge forces choices, why does God command us to choose?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: God foreknows but does not compel.

title: Does God Love Some People Less?
objection_text: If not everyone is saved, God must love some people less or not at all.
their_verses_text: Rom 9:13 – “Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated.”
tier1_summary: Biblical ‘hate’ often means preference for a mission, not lack of love.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 3:16 – God loves the world. 1 Tim 2 – God desires all saved.
tier2_short: God’s universal love is affirmed in Scripture. Romans 9 refers to vocational election, not personal salvation. God wills salvation for all, though He respects human freedom.
tier2_explanation: Election for mission is not unequal divine love.
tier3_discussion: If God loves only some, why does Scripture say He desires all to be saved?
patristic_refs: St. John Chrysostom: God’s love extends universally.

title: Is Salvation a One-Time Event?
objection_text: Salvation happens instantly the moment you accept Christ—nothing more is needed.
their_verses_text: Jn 5:24 – Whoever believes has eternal life.
tier1_summary: Scripture speaks of salvation as past, present, and future.
tier1_verses_text: Phil 2 – Work out salvation. Mt 24 – He who endures will be saved.
tier2_short: The New Testament describes salvation as a journey involving initial conversion, ongoing growth, and final perseverance. A single moment of belief does not replace lifelong discipleship.
tier2_explanation: Salvation unfolds over time as we persevere in faith.
tier3_discussion: If salvation is instant, why does Scripture speak of future salvation?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: We are saved in hope and perseverance.

title: Does God Forgive Without Repentance?
objection_text: God’s mercy is unconditional—repentance is unnecessary for forgiveness.
their_verses_text: Lk 6:36 – Be merciful as your Father is merciful.
tier1_summary: Jesus joins mercy with repentance, not replacement of it.
tier1_verses_text: Lk 13 – Unless you repent, you perish. Acts 2 – Repent and be baptized.
tier2_short: Mercy is not automatic forgiveness. Scripture consistently teaches that repentance opens the heart to God’s forgiveness and restoration. Mercy invites conversion, not indifference to sin.
tier2_explanation: Mercy calls us to repentance; it does not bypass it.
tier3_discussion: If forgiveness is automatic, why did Jesus command repentance repeatedly?
patristic_refs: St. Clement: Repentance restores us to God.

title: Can Good Works Earn Salvation?
objection_text: If Catholics emphasize works, they must believe works earn heaven.
their_verses_text: Isa 64:6 – All our righteous acts are like filthy rags.
tier1_summary: Good works flow from grace—they don’t cause it.
tier1_verses_text: Eph 2 – Created for good works. Jas 2 – Not by faith alone.
tier2_short: Catholics reject works‑righteousness. All good works come from grace and are our cooperation with God’s initiative. Works done in grace do not earn salvation but participate in it.
tier2_explanation: Grace produces good works; works don’t produce grace.
tier3_discussion: If works earn salvation, why does the Church teach salvation is a gift?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: God crowns His own gifts in us.

title: Does Suicide Automatically Damn a Person?
objection_text: Taking your own life is a mortal sin that guarantees hell.
their_verses_text: Ex 20:13 – You shall not kill.
tier1_summary: Grave matter requires full knowledge and deliberate consent.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 8 – Do not condemn without knowledge. Ps 34 – God is close to the brokenhearted.
tier2_short: Suicide is grave, but psychological factors may lessen culpability. The Church entrusts such souls to God’s mercy and never declares individual damnation.
tier2_explanation: Psychological distress may reduce personal guilt; God alone judges.
tier3_discussion: If suicide guarantees hell, why does the Church pray for those who die this way?
patristic_refs: No direct patristic teaching; aligns with broader mercy tradition.

title: Does Baptism Guarantee Salvation?
objection_text: If infants or adults are baptized, aren’t they automatically saved?
their_verses_text: 1 Pt 3:21 – Baptism now saves you.
tier1_summary: Baptism initiates salvation, but one must persevere in grace.
tier1_verses_text: Heb 6 – Some fall away after enlightenment. Gal 5 – You have fallen from grace.
tier2_short: Baptism truly saves, but salvation entails ongoing cooperation with God. Grace received can be lost through grave sin or rejection of faith.
tier2_explanation: Baptism begins salvation; it must be lived out.
tier3_discussion: If baptism guarantees salvation, why does Scripture speak of falling from grace?
patristic_refs: St. Cyril of Jerusalem: Baptism is the beginning of illumination.

title: Is Salvation Possible Without Forgiving Others?
objection_text: God forgives us even if we don’t forgive others.
their_verses_text: Ps 103:12 – God removes our sins far from us.
tier1_summary: Jesus explicitly ties our forgiveness to forgiving others.
tier1_verses_text: Mt 6 – God forgives us as we forgive. Mt 18 – The unforgiving servant condemned.
tier2_short: God’s mercy transforms us so we become merciful. Refusing forgiveness contradicts the grace we receive and closes the heart to God’s mercy.
tier2_explanation: We must extend the mercy God gives us; refusing forgiveness blocks grace.
tier3_discussion: If forgiveness is optional, why did Jesus make it a condition for receiving mercy?
patristic_refs: St. Cyprian: We cannot seek what we deny to others.

title: Is Faith Alone Enough Without Obedience?
objection_text: Once you believe, obedience is optional—salvation is already secured.
their_verses_text: Jn 3:16 – Whoever believes has eternal life.
tier1_summary: Believing in Jesus includes obeying His commandments.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 14:15 – If you love me, keep my commands. Heb 5:9 – Salvation to those who obey.
tier2_short: Biblical faith is relational trust expressed through obedience. Saving faith is never mere intellectual assent but living, active fidelity.
tier2_explanation: True faith produces obedience; disobedience rejects grace.
tier3_discussion: If obedience is irrelevant, why does Scripture link salvation to obedience?
patristic_refs: St. James: Faith without works is dead.

title: Does God Predestine Some People to Hell?
objection_text: Double predestination is biblical—God creates some people only for damnation.
their_verses_text: Rom 9:22 – Vessels of wrath prepared for destruction.
tier1_summary: Scripture affirms God desires all to be saved.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Tim 2 – God wills all saved. Ez 33 – God takes no pleasure in the death of the wicked.
tier2_short: Romans 9 addresses God’s patience and human resistance, not predestining anyone to hell. Damnation results from freely rejecting grace.
tier2_explanation: God predestines no one to hell; damnation is freely chosen.
tier3_discussion: If God predestines people to hell, why does He plead with them to return?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: God predestines no one to evil.

title: Does Suffering Mean God Has Rejected You?
objection_text: If God loved you, you wouldn’t suffer—suffering means abandonment.
their_verses_text: Ps 22:1 – “My God, why have you forsaken me?”
tier1_summary: Scripture shows suffering unites us more deeply to Christ.
tier1_verses_text: Rom 8 – We suffer with Christ to be glorified. Jas 1 – Trials build endurance.
tier2_short: Suffering is not a sign of abandonment but participation in Christ’s redemptive work. God uses suffering to strengthen virtue and deepen union with Him.
tier2_explanation: Suffering purifies and unites us to Christ, not proof of rejection.
tier3_discussion: If suffering proves God rejects us, why did Christ suffer most of all?
patristic_refs: St. Ignatius of Antioch: Suffering joins us to Christ.

title: Can Someone Lose Salvation by Accident?
objection_text: You might unintentionally commit a sin that causes loss of salvation.
their_verses_text: Jas 3:2 – We all stumble in many ways.
tier1_summary: Mortal sin requires full knowledge and deliberate consent.
tier1_verses_text: 1 Jn 5 – Sin unto death. Lk 12 – Degrees of responsibility.
tier2_short: Salvation cannot be lost by accident or momentary weakness. Only deliberate, grave rejection of God ruptures the life of grace.
tier2_explanation: Salvation is lost only through deliberate, grave sin—not accident.
tier3_discussion: If salvation can be lost accidentally, why does Scripture define mortal sin as deliberate?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Sin requires the will.

title: Does God Save People Without Any Human Cooperation?
objection_text: Grace alone means humans do nothing—God saves entirely without cooperation.
their_verses_text: Eph 2:8–9 – Saved by grace, not works.
tier1_summary: Grace invites and empowers cooperation, not passivity.
tier1_verses_text: Phil 2 – God works in us. 2 Pt 1 – Add virtue, self-control, perseverance.
tier2_short: Grace is God’s initiative, but Scripture teaches we must respond freely. Cooperation does not compete with grace; it expresses it.
tier2_explanation: Grace enables our cooperation; it doesn’t replace it.
tier3_discussion: If humans do nothing, why does Scripture command spiritual growth?
patristic_refs: St. Gregory: Grace perfects our effort.

title: Does God Punish People for Sins They Inherited?
objection_text: Original sin is unfair—God wouldn’t condemn people for Adam’s sin.
their_verses_text: Ez 18:20 – The son shall not bear the guilt of the father.
tier1_summary: Original sin is a wounded condition, not personal guilt.
tier1_verses_text: Rom 5 – Death spread to all. 1 Cor 15 – In Adam all die; in Christ all live.
tier2_short: Original sin doesn’t mean personal fault. It describes humanity’s inherited separation from grace. Salvation restores what was lost—not punishment for Adam.
tier2_explanation: Original sin is a condition we inherit, not guilt we committed.
tier3_discussion: If original sin is unjust, why does Scripture contrast Adam and Christ?
patristic_refs: St. Irenaeus: Adam’s fall wounded all humanity.

title: Catholics teach works-based salvation.
objection_text: Catholics teach works-based salvation.
tier2_short: Catholics do not believe works earn salvation; salvation begins and ends with God’s grace.
tier2_explanation: Catholics believe we are saved by grace and empowered by the Holy Spirit to live out that grace. Works are the fruit of salvation, not its cause. Scripture links faith and obedience, and the early Church rejected any idea of self-earned salvation.
patristic_refs: Augustine, On Grace and Free Will

title: Faith alone saves — Scripture says so.
objection_text: Faith alone saves — Scripture says so.
tier2_short: The only place Scripture uses 'faith alone' rejects it.
tier2_explanation: James 2:24 explicitly says we are 'not justified by faith alone.' Scripture teaches that saving faith is living, obedient, and united to love. Paul condemns works of the law, not works of grace. Early Christians never taught salvation by faith alone.
patristic_refs: Clement of Rome, Letter to Corinthians

title: Is Hell Incompatible With a Loving God?
objection_text: A loving God would not allow eternal suffering—hell cannot be real.
their_verses_text: 1 Jn 4:8 – God is love.
tier1_summary: Love respects freedom—even the freedom to reject God.
tier1_verses_text: Mt 25 – Eternal punishment. Mt 23 – Jesus laments those unwilling to come.
tier2_short: Hell exists because love requires freedom. God invites but does not force salvation. Eternal separation is the result of freely rejecting grace.
tier2_explanation: Hell is chosen separation from God, not imposed cruelty.
tier3_discussion: If love eliminates hell, why did Jesus warn about it more than anyone?
patristic_refs: St. Augustine: Hell is what the will chooses against God.

title: Catholics add to the finished work of Christ.
objection_text: Catholics add to the finished work of Christ.
tier2_short: Christ’s sacrifice is perfect; we participate in it, not add to it.
tier2_explanation: Catholics believe Christ’s work is complete and all-sufficient. Our role is to receive and cooperate with that grace. Scripture says we share in Christ’s sufferings and obedience, not because His work is lacking, but because He draws believers into His saving life.
patristic_refs: Irenaeus, Against Heresies

title: Can People Be Saved by Being ‘Good Without Religion’?
objection_text: You don’t need God—just be a good person and live morally.
their_verses_text: Rom 2:14 – Gentiles do the law by nature.
tier1_summary: Natural goodness prepares the heart, but salvation comes from Christ.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 14 – No one comes to the Father except through Christ. Acts 4 – Salvation in no other name.
tier2_short: Human goodness is valuable but cannot replace grace. Salvation is a divine gift through Christ, even for those who know Him imperfectly.
tier2_explanation: Goodness helps us respond to grace but cannot save without Christ.
tier3_discussion: If goodness alone saves, why did Christ come at all?
patristic_refs: St. Justin: Truth seeds prepare hearts for Christ.

title: Is Fear of Hell Manipulative?
objection_text: Religion uses fear of hell to control people—God wouldn’t want fear involved.
their_verses_text: 1 Jn 4:18 – Perfect love casts out fear.
tier1_summary: Holy fear is the beginning of wisdom and leads to love.
tier1_verses_text: Prov 9 – Fear of the Lord is wisdom. Phil 2 – Work out salvation with fear.
tier2_short: Fear of separation from God is not manipulation; it is realism. Scripture affirms holy fear as a starting point that matures into perfect love.
tier2_explanation: Holy fear protects us until love is perfected.
tier3_discussion: If fear is always bad, why does Scripture call it the beginning of wisdom?
patristic_refs: St. Basil: Fear leads us to virtue until love matures.

title: Grace is a free gift — why must we cooperate?
objection_text: Grace is a free gift — why must we cooperate?
tier2_short: Grace is free, but love requires a response.
tier2_explanation: God initiates salvation purely by grace, but He never forces our participation. Cooperation simply means responding freely to what God already gives. Scripture calls believers coworkers with God, and Augustine taught that even our cooperation is empowered by grace.
patristic_refs: Augustine, Letters 186

title: You cannot lose salvation (Once Saved Always Saved).
objection_text: You cannot lose salvation (Once Saved Always Saved).
tier2_short: Scripture repeatedly warns believers not to fall away.
tier2_explanation: Jesus and the apostles warn that believers can turn from grace through serious sin. Salvation is secure in Christ, but not unconditional. Early Christians taught perseverance, not guaranteed security, and Scripture shows real examples of believers who fell from faith.
patristic_refs: Tertullian, On Modesty

title: Does God Only Save Those With Strong Faith?
objection_text: Weak or inconsistent faith means you are not truly saved.
their_verses_text: Jas 1:6 – Ask without doubting.
tier1_summary: Even weak faith, if genuine, opens us to God’s grace.
tier1_verses_text: Mk 9 – “Help my unbelief!” Mt 17 – Faith like a mustard seed.
tier2_short: God strengthens weak faith rather than rejecting it. Scripture shows that even minimal trust, when sincere, allows grace to grow within us.
tier2_explanation: God welcomes imperfect faith and helps it mature.
tier3_discussion: If only strong faith saves, why did Jesus praise mustard-seed faith?
patristic_refs: St. Gregory: God crowns even small beginnings.

title: Does God Reject People Who Doubt?
objection_text: Doubt proves you lack real faith and are unsaved.
their_verses_text: Jas 1:6–7 – Doubters should not expect anything from God.
tier1_summary: Scripture distinguishes honest doubt from refusal to trust.
tier1_verses_text: Jn 20 – Jesus invites Thomas to believe. Jude – Have mercy on the doubting.
tier2_short: Doubt often coexists with faith and can lead to deeper trust. God responds to honest seeking with mercy, not rejection.
tier2_explanation: Honest doubt invites God’s mercy; obstinate refusal is different.
tier3_discussion: If doubt proved damnation, why did Jesus gently guide Thomas?
patristic_refs: St. Gregory: God works through imperfect belief.

title: Purgatory denies the sufficiency of the Cross.
objection_text: Purgatory denies the sufficiency of the Cross.
tier2_short: Purgatory applies Christ’s finished work; it doesn’t replace it.
tier2_explanation: Purgatory is not a second chance but the final purification Christ’s grace accomplishes. His sacrifice fully saves us, yet holiness still needs completion for those who die imperfectly purified. Early Christians prayed for the dead, showing belief in post-death purification.
patristic_refs: Augustine, City of God 21.13

title: ‘Catholics say we earn heaven through merit.’
objection_text: ‘Catholics say we earn heaven through merit.’
tier2_short: Catholics don’t earn heaven; grace produces merit within God’s family.
tier2_explanation: Merit in Catholic teaching is simply God crowning His own gifts. Grace makes our actions pleasing to Him, but they never earn salvation. Scripture shows God rewarding works done in grace, and early Christians saw merit as participation, not self-achievement.
patristic_refs: Augustine, On Grace and Free Will

title: Are People Saved by Saying the ‘Sinner’s Prayer’?
objection_text: If you say the sinner’s prayer sincerely, you are saved forever.
their_verses_text: Rom 10:9 – Confess and believe, and you will be saved.
tier1_summary: Confession is the beginning of salvation, not its completion.
tier1_verses_text: Mt 7 – Not everyone who says ‘Lord’ will enter. Jas 2 – Not by faith alone.
tier2_short: Sincere confession is vital, but Scripture teaches ongoing conversion, obedience, sacramental life, and perseverance—not a one-time prayer—as the path to salvation.
tier2_explanation: The sinner’s prayer begins faith; it does not replace discipleship.
tier3_discussion: If one prayer guarantees salvation, why does Jesus warn about false professions?
patristic_refs: St. Justin: Faith leads to transformation and obedience.

title: ‘Why confess to a priest if Jesus already paid for all sins?’
objection_text: ‘Why confess to a priest if Jesus already paid for all sins?’
tier2_short: Confession applies Christ’s forgiveness personally and sacramentally.
tier2_explanation: Jesus gave the apostles authority to forgive sins, making confession a continuation of His ministry. Confession doesn’t replace Christ’s work—it applies it. Early Christians confessed to bishops or priests, showing it was part of the Church’s original practice.
patristic_refs: Cyprian, The Lapsed

title: ‘It is finished — so why sacraments?’
objection_text: ‘It is finished — so why sacraments?’
tier2_short: Christ’s sacrifice is complete; sacraments make its grace present to us.
tier2_explanation: ‘It is finished’ refers to Christ’s saving work, not an end to all means of grace. Sacraments apply that finished work to believers. Early Christians understood sacraments as living channels of the grace Christ won on the Cross.
patristic_refs: Irenaeus, Against Heresies

title: ‘Why is baptism necessary for salvation?’
objection_text: ‘Why is baptism necessary for salvation?’
tier2_short: Because Jesus taught new birth comes through water and Spirit.
tier2_explanation: Scripture consistently links baptism to salvation, forgiveness, and new birth. It is God’s action, not ours. The early Church universally treated baptism as necessary for entering the Christian life and receiving the fullness of grace.
patristic_refs: Justin Martyr, First Apology

title: ‘All sin is equal — so why mortal vs venial?’
objection_text: ‘All sin is equal — so why mortal vs venial?’
tier2_short: Jesus and Scripture distinguish between serious and lesser sins.
tier2_explanation: The Bible identifies sin leading to death and sin not leading to death (1 John 5). Mortal sin destroys charity; venial sin wounds it. Early Christians and councils consistently taught degrees of sin with different effects on the soul.
patristic_refs: Augustine, Homilies on John

title: ‘If we’re saved by grace, why judgment according to works?’
objection_text: ‘If we’re saved by grace, why judgment according to works?’
tier2_short: Works reveal the reality of our faith.
tier2_explanation: Judgment by works doesn’t contradict salvation by grace. Scripture teaches that works reveal whether grace has taken root. Early Christians saw final judgment as evaluating the fruit of grace, not human achievement.
patristic_refs: Clement of Rome, Letter to Corinthians

title: Why does God judge people if salvation is grace?
objection_text: Why does God judge people if salvation is grace?
tier2_short: God judges how we responded to His grace.
tier2_explanation: Judgment is not God weighing achievements—it reveals whether grace transformed us. Scripture shows God evaluating our response to His gifts. Early Christians saw judgment as confirming the soul’s orientation toward or away from God’s offered grace.
patristic_refs: Origen, On First Principles

title: Predestination means free will doesn’t matter.
objection_text: Predestination means free will doesn’t matter.
tier2_short: God’s foreknowledge doesn’t cancel human freedom.
tier2_explanation: Catholics reject fatalistic predestination. God’s eternal plan includes our free cooperation. Scripture shows God desires all to be saved and invites real choice. Early Christians taught synergy—God initiates, humans freely respond without compulsion.
patristic_refs: John Chrysostom, Homilies on Romans

title: If God wants all to be saved, why aren’t all saved?
objection_text: If God wants all to be saved, why aren’t all saved?
tier2_short: Because God respects human freedom.
tier2_explanation: God truly desires all to be saved, but He will not force love. Scripture shows people can resist His call. Early Christians taught salvation requires cooperation with grace, preserving both God’s will and human dignity.
patristic_refs: Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho

title: Does suffering mean I’m not saved yet?
objection_text: Does suffering mean I’m not saved yet?
tier2_short: Suffering unites us to Christ; it’s not proof of rejection.
tier2_explanation: Scripture teaches suffering refines believers and unites them to Christ’s saving work. It is not a sign of rejection but purification. Early Christians saw trials as participation in Christ’s life, not evidence of God’s absence.
patristic_refs: Ignatius of Antioch, Letter to Romans

title: Why do Catholics say salvation is a process?
objection_text: Why do Catholics say salvation is a process?
tier2_short: Because Scripture describes salvation as past, present, and future.
tier2_explanation: The Bible says we have been saved, are being saved, and will be saved. Salvation begins with grace, grows through discipleship, and ends in glory. Early Christians saw salvation as transformation, not a one‑time declaration.
patristic_refs: Irenaeus, Against Heresies

title: Does Hebrews 10 teach losing salvation by sin?
objection_text: Does Hebrews 10 teach losing salvation by sin?
tier2_short: Hebrews warns about rejecting grace, not minor failings.
tier2_explanation: Hebrews 10 describes deliberate rejection of Christ after receiving truth. It warns about apostasy, not ordinary failings. Early Christians saw this as a sober reminder that grace can be resisted and abandoned through willful rebellion.
patristic_refs: Tertullian, On Modesty

title: Does Romans 8 mean salvation can’t be lost?
objection_text: Does Romans 8 mean salvation can’t be lost?
tier2_short: Romans 8 promises God’s faithfulness—not automatic perseverance.
tier2_explanation: Paul lists external forces that cannot separate us from God, but he never denies our freedom to reject grace through serious sin. Early Christians taught that apostasy, not outside threats, endangers salvation. Perseverance is a gift we must cooperate with, not a guarantee.
patristic_refs: Origen, Commentary on Romans

title: If salvation requires obedience, isn’t that works?
objection_text: If salvation requires obedience, isn’t that works?
tier2_short: Obedience is faith in action—not a separate work.
